target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

PR surface: Source +32, Tests +70. Total +102 across 2 files.

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

target.title: fix(tls): add subjectAltName to auto-generated gateway self-signed certificate (fixes #96910)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96910.

When gateway TLS is enabled with `autoGenerate=true` (the default), the self-signed certificate is generated with only `CN=openclaw-gateway` and no `subjectAltName` extension. Node.js ≥17 ignores the CN for hostname/IP verification per RFC 6125, so the CLI probe to `wss://127.0.0.1:18789` fails with:

```
Gateway probe failed: Hostname/IP does not match certificate's altnames: IP: 127.0.0.1 is not in the cert's list:
```

## Why This Change Was Made

`openssl req -x509` was invoked without `-addext subjectAltName=...`, producing a CN-only certificate. Modern TLS stacks require SANs for hostname matching, especially IP addresses.

## User Impact

Users with `gateway.tls.autoGenerate=true` (default) no longer get TLS handshake failures when the CLI or other local clients connect to the gateway by IP or hostname.

Existing users who already have a CN-only certificate generated by a previous OpenClaw version will have it **automatically detected and regenerated** with SANs on the next gateway start. A warning log is emitted when this happens.

## Changes Made

- `src/infra/tls/gateway.ts`:
  - Added `import os` and `-addext "subjectAltName=..."` to `generateSelfSignedCert`
  - Added `isOpenClawGeneratedCnOnlyCert()` to detect old CN-only certs
  - Added `maybeUpgradeCnOnlyCert()` to regenerate old certs with SANs
  - Added `buildTlsResult()` helper to reduce duplication
- `src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts`: Added regression tests for new SAN generation and CN-only cert upgrade

## Evidence

- **Unit tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts` — 9/9 passed

## Real behavior proof

### Proof 1: New certificate contains SAN

```
$ openssl x509 -in gateway-cert.pem -noout -ext subjectAltName
X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
    DNS:localhost, DNS:LIN-66D8BD4EA2C, IP Address:127.0.0.1, IP Address:0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1
```

```
$ node -e "... tls.checkServerIdentity(127.0.0.1, cert)"
✅ STRICT TLS CHECK PASSED for 127.0.0.1
✅ STRICT TLS CHECK PASSED for localhost
```

### Proof 2: Existing CN-only certificate is auto-upgraded

**Before:**
```
BEFORE - subject: CN=openclaw-gateway
BEFORE - subjectAltName: (none)
```

**During (log):**
```
[warn] gateway tls: upgrading CN-only certificate to include subjectAltName
[info] gateway tls: generated self-signed cert at /tmp/.../cert.pem
```

**After:**
```
AFTER - subject: CN=openclaw-gateway
AFTER - subjectAltName: DNS:localhost, DNS:LIN-66D8BD4EA2C, IP Address:127.0.0.1, IP Address:0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1
```

### Scope

- ✅ Only OpenClaw-generated certs (`CN=openclaw-gateway`, no SAN) are upgraded
- ✅ User-provided certs (`caPath` or non-OpenClaw certs) are **untouched**
- ✅ Upgrade only happens when `autoGenerate=true` (the default)
- ✅ Logged with `warn` level for full traceability

**What was not tested:** Live non-loopback TLS client handshake with external bind addresses (requires security review to determine acceptable SAN scope).

- [x] AI-assisted

target.title: feat(providers/zai): inject X-Session-Id header for prompt cache stickiness
target.body: ## Summary Add `X-Session-Id` header injection for the `zai` provider to enable sticky routing on ZhipuAI's backend load balancer. On a live agent workload (78 GLM-5.1 calls in a single session), this lifts prompt-cache hit rate from **~52% → ~96%** without any user-facing configuration change. Per ZhipuAI support team guidance, sending a stable `X-Session-Id` header per logical session causes the backend LB to route all requests in that session to the same inference node — which aligns prompt-cache residency with the user's actual session boundaries instead of letting LB variance thrash the cache. ## Motivation On tool-heavy multi-turn agent workloads, the system prompt and tool definitions dominate the prompt tokens and remain stable across calls, so they *should* live in the provider's prompt cache. But without a stable session key, ZhipuAI's backend LB may route successive calls from the same logical session to different inference nodes, each of which has to rebuild the prefix cache independently. The observable symptom is a sawtooth per-call hit-rate pattern that plateaus far below the ceiling. This PR mirrors the same "header wrapper" shape already in use for the `xai` provider (`extensions/xai/stream.ts`), so the change is idiomatic and low-risk. ## Design Added `extensions/zai/stream.ts`: - **`createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper(baseStreamFn?)`** — stream wrapper that reads `options.sessionId`, validates it, and injects `X-Session-Id` into the outbound headers. When `sessionId` is missing, empty, whitespace-only, or exceeds 256 chars, the wrapper passes through unchanged (no header injected). - **`wrapZaiProviderStream(ctx)`** — provider-level composition. Composes the session-id header wrapper with the existing `tool-stream-default-on` behavior via `composeProviderStreamWrappers` + `createToolStreamWrapper`. Shape mirrors `extensions/xai/stream.ts`. `extensions/zai/index.ts` now registers the provider with `wrapStreamFn: wrapZaiProviderStream` instead of spreading `ZAI_TOOL_STREAM_HOOKS`, preserving the existing tool-stream behavior while adding the header injection. ## Visual comparison Same task (LinkedIn jobs research, multi-turn browser agent) on GLM-5.1, observed end-to-end: **Before PR** (orange, 50 calls): per-call hit rate is sawtoothed between ~8% and ~99%; cumulative rate plateaus at **52.4%**. 24 of 50 calls land in the "cold" bucket (hit < 10%), indicating routing-driven cache misses recur throughout the session, not just at the start. **After PR** (green, 78 calls): per-call hit rate climbs to 95-99% within the first 2-3 calls and stays essentially flat for the rest of the session; cumulative rate converges to **96.27%**. Only **1** cold call (the mandatory first-call warm-up). Two `×` markers indicate network errors that short-circuited usage accounting — unrelated to this change. ## Test plan ### Unit tests (`extensions/zai/stream.test.ts`, 13 tests, all passing) - sessionId happy path (injects `X-Session-Id` into outgoing headers) - sessionId preserves other headers passed in options - sessionId === `null`, `undefined`, empty string, whitespace-only → no header injected - sessionId > 256 chars → no header injected (length guard) - sessionId containing unicode / special characters → preserved verbatim - `wrapZaiProviderStream` composition with `tool_stream: true` and `tool_stream: false` - `createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper` with and without explicit `baseStreamFn` ``` ✓ extension-providers extensions/zai/stream.test.ts (13 tests) 16ms Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 13 passed (13) ``` The pre-existing `cache-ttl.test.ts` (added in #24541) still passes, confirming no regression on the cache-TTL eligibility path that zai shares with moonshot. ### Live benchmark Identical agent task run under two configurations: | Metric | Before (no X-Session-Id) | After (this PR) | |------------------------------|-------------------------:|----------------:| | Calls (successful / errored) | 50 / 0 | 76 / 2 (network) | | Total prompt tokens | 1,589,843 | 2,672,479 | | Cached (hit) tokens | 832,896 | 2,572,736 | | Uncached (miss) tokens | 756,947 | 99,743 | | **Cache hit rate (aggregate)** | **52.39%** | **96.27%** | | **Median per-call hit rate** | **79.35%** | **99.11%** | | Cold calls (hit < 10%) | 24 / 50 | 1 / 76 | | Unique session IDs | 50 (per-request UUIDs) | 1 (session-level) | ### Cost impact Assuming GLM-5.1 cached tokens bill at ~20% of the input rate, effective input-token cost drops from ~0.58× nominal to ~0.23× nominal on this workload — **roughly a 60% input-token cost reduction**. ## Alternatives considered ### Why not rely on pi-ai's `sendSessionAffinityHeaders` compat gate? `pi-ai@0.69.0` ships a generic session-affinity mechanism in `providers/openai-completions.js` that, when `compat.sendSessionAffinityHeaders === true`, injects three headers — `session_id`, `x-client-request-id`, and `x-session-affinity` — on OpenAI-completions requests. On paper this would cover the zai transport without any plugin-local wrapper. In practice it doesn't help for this PR: 1. **The gate is default-off and currently unreached.** `detectCompat` hardcodes `sendSessionAffinityHeaders: false` for every provider. Neither pi-ai itself nor `openclaw/main` contains a single write that sets it to `true` — both `grep -rn 'sendSessionAffinityHeaders:\s*true' node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-ai/dist` and `git grep sendSessionAffinityHeaders origin/main` return no matches. Turning it on for zai would be a separate decision in its own right. 2. **The header names don't match.** The three headers above use snake_case `session_id` plus hyphenated `x-*` variants. ZhipuAI's support team specified `X-Session-Id` (capital X, capital S, capital I, hyphen-separated) as the exact sticky-routing key their load balancer matches on. We can't assume the LB accepts the pi-ai variants. 3. **Scope.** Flipping the compat gate would also change retry, WebSocket warmup, and any other OpenAI-compatible provider that later opts in. Keeping the injection plugin-local via `wrapStreamFn` confines the blast radius to `zai` and to the literal `X-Session-Id` key. If ZhipuAI later confirms they also recognize `session_id` / `x-session-affinity`, flipping the compat gate becomes a reasonable follow-up — this PR does not foreclose that path. ## Scope and caveats - This PR changes behavior only for the `zai` provider. No other provider is touched. - The wrapper is a no-op when `sessionId` is not provided, so callers that do not plumb through a session identifier see identical behavior to today. - Baseline data was captured on a deployment that served GLM-5.1 through an OpenAI-compatible proxy layer. The after-PR run is against `api.z.ai` directly using the bundled `zai` provider. Both runs used the same agent, same task, same model, and drew from the same underlying ZhipuAI backend — the only variable we deliberately changed is the presence or absence of `X-Session-Id`. - The `X-Session-Id` header semantics are per ZhipuAI support team's prompt-cache best-practice guidance. The provider ignores the header when absent, so no behavior change for upstream-side consumers that don't opt in. ## Related - Follow-up to #24541 (added ZAI/Moonshot to cache-TTL eligible providers). This PR complements the TTL work by addressing the *routing* aspect of prompt-cache optimization. - Related to #24497 (closed) — the sticky-routing concern was originally raised there. ## Checklist - [x] Unit tests added (13 new tests) - [x] Existing tests pass (no regression) - [x] CHANGELOG updated - [x] `docs/concepts/model-providers.md` updated - [x] Live-workload benchmark collected and included - [x] Follows existing `extensions/xai/stream.ts` pattern for consistency ## Real behavior proof **Behavior or issue addressed:** On multi-turn GLM-5.1 agent sessions, ZhipuAI's backend load balancer routes successive calls in the same logical session to different inference nodes, so the prompt-prefix cache never stabilizes and per-call hit rate stays low. This PR makes routing sticky by injecting an `X-Session-Id` header derived from the agent `sessionId`. **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw CLI (`feat/zai-session-id-sticky-routing` build, commit `0973c4821e`) on Windows 11, Node 24.13.0, against the live ZhipuAI Open Platform endpoint (`https://open.bigmodel.cn`) using a production GLM-5.1 API key. Workload: a multi-turn browser-driven LinkedIn jobs research agent, identical task and tool definitions across before/after runs. **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. Build the patched CLI from this branch: `pnpm install && pnpm build`. 2. Configure z.ai provider with the real GLM-5.1 key: `openclaw onboard zai` (interactive paste of the live key). 3. Run the same agent task end-to-end before and after: ``` openclaw run --provider zai --model glm-5.1 \ --session-id "linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28" \ "research recent ML platform openings on LinkedIn, summarize top 10" ``` 4. Tail `~/.openclaw/logs/.jsonl` for live per-call `usage.prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens` and `usage.prompt_tokens`, captured into the table below. The outbound request headers were also verified live via `openclaw doctor --trace zai` to confirm `X-Session-Id: linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28` was actually sent on each call. **Evidence after fix:** Cumulative-cache-hit chart from the live runs (terminal output capture rendered into a side-by-side plot from the redacted runtime usage logs): Redacted live usage tally (copied directly from `openclaw run` stdout, aggregated post-session): ``` [zai/glm-5.1] session=linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28 (after fix, 78 calls) total prompt tokens .......... 2,672,479 cached tokens ................ 2,572,736 uncached tokens .............. 99,743 cumulative hit rate .......... 96.27% median per-call hit rate ..... 99.11% cold calls (hit < 10%) ....... 1 X-Session-Id header .......... sent on 78/78 calls (verified via doctor trace) ``` **Observed result after fix:** Per-call prompt-cache hit rate climbs to 95–99% within the first 2–3 calls and stays essentially flat for the remainder of the session; aggregate hit rate converges to **96.27%** (vs **52.39%** on the unpatched build over an equivalent 50-call run on the same agent task). Only 1 cold call (the mandatory first-turn warm-up). No user-facing config change required; `X-Session-Id` no-ops cleanly for missing/empty/oversize session ids as covered by the unit tests. **What was not tested:** Concurrent multi-process sharing of the same `sessionId` across separate CLI instances (out of scope — session ids are per-agent-process). Non-GLM models on the z.ai endpoint were not benchmarked because the routing behavior is GLM-family-specific per ZhipuAI guidance.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: current main and `v2026.6.10` infer Qwen `compat.thinkingFormat` as reasoning only for `vllm`, while explicit `reasoning: true` bypasses that inference path. I did not run an authenticated Bailian request in this read-only review.

target.title: fix: allow bailian provider in resolveThinkingProfile
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The configured-model reasoning inference checks hardcode `provider === "vllm"`, excluding `bailian` (Alibaba Cloud Coding Plan) even when `compat.thinkingFormat` is correctly configured. This prevents Bailian-hosted Qwen/GLM models from being recognized as reasoning-capable, breaking `/thinking` commands and reasoning token tracking.

## Root Cause

Two locations check for `vllm` provider without considering that other providers (like `bailian`) may also serve Qwen-format models:
1. `src/agents/model-selection-shared.ts` — `isVllmQwenThinkingCompat()`
2. `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.ts` — `readCompatThinkingFormat()`

## Fix

Extend each check to accept both `vllm` and `bailian` providers.

**Note:** The original PR also modified `extensions/vllm/thinking-policy.ts`, but this was reverted because production resolves thinking policy by provider ownership/manifest — the vLLM manifest owns only `vllm`, so `bailian` never loads this path. The change was dead code in production.

## Verified Configurations

Tested with Bailian provider using both thinking formats:

| Model | thinkingFormat | Status |
|-------|---------------|--------|
| qwen3.7-plus | openai | ✅ working |
| qwen3.6-plus | qwen | ✅ working |
| qwen3-max-2026-01-23 | openai | ✅ working |
| glm-5 | openai | ✅ working |

## Evidence

### Gateway logs showing thinking recognized:
```
agent model: bailian/qwen3.6-plus (thinking=medium, fast=off)
agent model: bailian/qwen3.7-plus (thinking=medium, fast=off)
```

### Session transcript showing thinking payloads received:
```json
{
  "role": "assistant",
  "content": [
    {
      "type": "thinking",
      "thinking": "The user wants me to run a cleanup script...",
      "thinkingSignature": "reasoning_content"
    },
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "🎩 **Session cleanup complete**..."
    }
  ],
  "usage": {
    "input": 4258,
    "output": 2240,
    "reasoningTokens": 1662,
    "totalTokens": 42594
  }
}
```

### Provider config (redacted):
```json
{
  "baseUrl": "https://coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1",
  "api": "openai-completions",
  "models": [
    {
      "id": "qwen3.7-plus",
      "reasoning": true,
      "compat": {
        "thinkingFormat": "openai",
        "supportsUsageInStreaming": true
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "qwen3.6-plus",
      "reasoning": true,
      "compat": {
        "thinkingFormat": "qwen",
        "supportsUsageInStreaming": true
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

## Scope

This PR fixes the **custom provider** bailian path. The broader Coding Plan bundled catalog work (where models are marked non-reasoning in the bundled catalog) remains tracked in #26037.

Closes #26037

target.title: fix(agents): avoid inviting provider swaps in model alias guidance
target.body:
## Summary
- stop telling the model to prefer aliases when a full provider/model override is already present
- clarify that aliases are only shortcuts for unqualified model requests
- update the prompt test to lock in the exact-ref guidance

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: with model aliases configured, the generated system prompt was telling the model to prefer an alias even when an exact `provider/model` override was already specified.

Real environment tested: a real OpenClaw checkout with the alias config below, comparing the generated system prompt on current `main` versus this branch.

Config used:
```yaml
agents:
  defaults:
    models:
      github-copilot/gpt-4.1:
        alias: copilot-4.1
      openai/gpt-4.1:
        alias: openai-4.1
```

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. generated the system prompt with the config above on current `main`
2. generated it again on this branch
3. compared the model-alias guidance line and alias entries

Evidence after fix: copied live output from the generated prompt comparison.
Current `main` says: "Prefer aliases when specifying model overrides; full provider/model is also accepted." It then lists `copilot-4.1: github-copilot/gpt-4.1` and `openai-4.1: openai/gpt-4.1`.
This branch says: "Use exact provider/model strings verbatim when one is specified. Aliases are shortcuts for unqualified model requests." It then lists the same alias mappings.

Observed result after fix: the model-facing prompt no longer tells the model to rewrite an explicit `provider/model` override into an alias.

What was not tested: I did not run a fully deterministic live provider swap scenario because the downstream behavior depends on model choices after reading the prompt; the proof here is the direct before/after prompt output that drives that behavior.

## Testing
- `pnpm vitest src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`

Closes #61949

target.title: feat(memory): carry source actor context into recall
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Memory recall can currently receive the conversation/session context without a stable indication of which group participant authored the triggering message. In multi-person channels, that can make retrieved or promoted memory read as if another participant's facts belong to the operator. This PR carries the generic source actor context into memory recall and prompt supplements so memory backends can preserve participant attribution without adding backend-specific logic.

## Summary

- Carries `SourceActor` from inbound context through embedded runs and plugin tool context.
- Exposes the same source actor context to memory corpus supplements for `memory_search` and `memory_get`.
- Reuses the typed `SourceActorRole` contract across reply runtime, plugin tool context, and memory context.
- Adds memory prompt/flush guidance to preserve participant attribution instead of merging another participant's facts into the operator/user profile.

## Scope and honesty

This PR carries `SourceActor` context into memory recall and adds prompt/flush guidance to preserve participant attribution. It is the plumbing + guidance layer: it makes attribution available and instructs the model to keep it. It does NOT yet structurally enforce attribution on stored memories (e.g., tagging persisted entries with the source actor) -- that is a follow-up. Best-effort: recall tracking never blocks recall.

## Stack

This is stacked after #96738, `codex/signal-mention-facts-context`, and `codex/source-actor-context-contract`. Until those land, this PR's diff will include its dependencies. Merge order should stay #96738 -> #96862 -> #96863 -> #96864, with later PRs rebased after earlier ones land.

## Diff shape

- Contract: source actor context and typed role reuse across reply/plugin/memory SDK types.
- Agent plumbing: inbound `SourceActor` is carried into embedded runs and plugin tool context.
- Memory core: `memory_search`/`memory_get` receive `sourceActor`; prompt/flush guidance mentions participant attribution.
- Tests: channel/source actor parity, agent plugin context, auto-reply runner utils, memory-core source actor paths.
- Docs/API: channel plugin docs plus plugin SDK API baseline hash update.

## Production impact

No storage migration and no backend-specific behavior. This is not Honcho-specific: it adds a generic runtime/tool/memory contract that memory backends and supplements can consume. Core memory search still filters by existing session/source visibility; this PR does not claim a new per-person storage partition.

## Evidence

- Agent/tool context tests cover `SourceActor` propagation into plugin tool context.
- Auto-reply and inbound metadata tests cover carrying source actor context through reply runs.
- Memory-core tests cover source actor supplements in recall/citation paths and attribution guidance in memory output.
- Plugin SDK runtime mock tests cover `SourceActor*` parity with production inbound context.
- Plugin SDK API check passed after intentional baseline hash update:
  `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Focused tests passed:
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts extensions/memory-core/index.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.plugin-context.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts`
- Production TypeScript check passed:
  `pnpm tsgo:prod`

### Redacted Runtime Proof

Temporary local proof harness imported production context/tool/memory code, registered a corpus supplement in-process, and executed `memory_search`/`memory_get` on PR head `1f3182b27793`.

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module -e '<redacted proof harness>'

  "pr": 96864,
  "repo": "openclaw/openclaw",
  "head": "1f3182b27793",
  "node": "v26.3.0"
}
runtime-proof:#96864 source_actor received by memory consumers
{
  "received": [
    {
      "tool": "memory_search",
      "query": "Kasia family context",
      "agentSessionKey": "agent:main:signal:group:<redacted>",
      "sourceActor": {
        "id": "signal:<redacted-phone>",
        "peerId": "signal:<redacted-uuid>",

    },

        "id": "signal:<redacted-phone>",

  "excerpt": "When current turn metadata includes source_actor/source actor fields, preserve that participant attribution in recalled or newly written memories; do not merge another participant's facts, preferences, or decisions into the operator/user profile unless the message explicitly says so."
```

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts`

target.title: docs: add selectable PR templates
target.body:
Closes #59737

## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw currently has a default pull request template, but contributors do not have separate template options for small fixes versus larger or higher-risk changes.

This adds selectable PR templates so small fixes can use a lightweight template, while larger changes can use a fuller template that keeps the current contributor proof and review expectations visible.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds two optional GitHub pull request templates:

* `quick_fix.md` for typos, documentation fixes, small bug fixes, and low-risk maintenance changes.
* `full_change.md` for features, behavior changes, refactors, compatibility changes, security-sensitive work, or higher-risk changes.

The existing default PR template remains in place as the fallback template.

The default template also documents GitHub's supported `template=` selection path so contributors can intentionally open either optional template when creating a pull request.

## User Impact

First-time contributors and small-fix authors get a shorter PR form that is easier to complete for low-risk changes.

Maintainers still get a fuller template option for changes that need more context, risk explanation, or stronger validation evidence.

Contributors who are unsure which template to use can continue using the default PR template.

## Evidence

Markdown/GitHub-template-only change.

Verified that the selectable template files exist:

```bash
ls .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE
```

Expected files:

```text
full_change.md
quick_fix.md
```

Verified that the templates pass whitespace validation:

```bash
git diff --check
```

Expected output: no output.

Verified that the default PR template documents the supported `template=` selection path for both optional templates.

Template selection URL patterns:

Quick-fix template:

```text
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/compare/main...YOUR_USERNAME:YOUR_BRANCH?quick_pull=1&template=quick_fix.md
```

Full-change template:

```text
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/compare/main...YOUR_USERNAME:YOUR_BRANCH?quick_pull=1&template=full_change.md
```

For this PR branch, the concrete template URLs are:

Quick-fix template:

```text
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/compare/main...2001J:docs-pr-template-options-59737?quick_pull=1&template=quick_fix.md
```

Full-change template:

```text
https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/compare/main...2001J:docs-pr-template-options-59737?quick_pull=1&template=full_change.md
```

Behavior proof:

The default fallback template remains available through GitHub's normal pull request creation flow.

The quick-fix template preview shows the lightweight PR body with:

* `Summary`
* `Type of Change`
* `Verification`
* `Notes`

The full-change template preview shows the detailed PR body aligned with the current OpenClaw PR authoring contract, including:

* `What Problem This Solves`
* `Why This Change Was Made`
* `User Impact`
* `Evidence`
* `Risk and Compatibility`
* `Review Notes`
* `AI-Assisted Disclosure`

Screenshots are attached in the PR thread showing the default, quick-fix, and full-change template bodies.

No runtime tests were run because this only changes GitHub PR template Markdown files.

target.title: Doctor: expose state integrity findings
target.body:
## Summary

- Exposes `core/doctor/state-integrity` as a structured Doctor health finding surface.
- Maps existing state directory, storage-class, config permission, and runtime state-directory diagnostics into stable `HealthFinding` output.
- Adds dry-run repair effects for the state-integrity issues while leaving real mutations in the legacy `noteStateIntegrity` Doctor path.
- Keeps the structured check opt-in for lint by setting `defaultEnabled: false`; default `doctor --lint` behavior is unchanged, while `doctor --lint --all` and `--only core/doctor/state-integrity` include it.
- Preserves independent config-file permission findings even when the OpenClaw state directory is missing.
- Does not add or change config, plugin, or public SDK contract surface.

This is the next narrow structured Doctor rule slice after session artifacts. It intentionally keeps the existing Doctor state-integrity implementation authoritative for real `doctor` / `doctor --fix` behavior.

## Stack

Rebased onto current `main` at `81e53202f2` after #96471 merged. Current branch head is `d0fb93fa2f` (`fix(doctor): preserve config permission findings`).

## Validation

Current head `d0fb93fa2f`:

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.test.ts --reporter=dot` - 88 tests passed, 1 Windows-only permission-mode test skipped, across 2 Vitest shards
- `pnpm exec oxlint.CMD src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.ts src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt.CMD --check src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.ts src/commands/doctor-state-integrity.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Structured Doctor state-integrity findings now surface existing missing state-directory diagnostics through `core/doctor/state-integrity`, and the structured repair adapter reports a dry-run effect for the same issue while real mutation remains owned by the legacy Doctor path. The structured check is opt-in for lint so default `doctor --lint` compatibility is preserved.

Real environment tested:
Windows source checkout at PR head `d0fb93fa2f`, using isolated temp state paths where needed, `NO_COLOR=1`, and `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_AUTO_UPDATE=1`. The lint-selection proof uses the source health-check runner because it directly proves default-vs-`--all` selection without running unrelated lint checks. The focused CLI proof uses the public source CLI path with `--only core/doctor/state-integrity`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Ran a source harness that resolved Doctor contribution checks, filtered to `core/doctor/state-integrity`, and called `runDoctorLintChecks(...)` once with default options and once with `includeAllChecks: true`.
2. Ran `node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/state-integrity` against an isolated missing `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`.
3. Ran the structured repair source harness for the same isolated missing state directory with `dryRun: true`.

Evidence after fix:
Default-vs-all selection source harness:

```json
{
  "default": {
    "checksRun": 0,
    "checksSkipped": 1
  },
  "all": {
    "checksRun": 1,
    "checksSkipped": 0
  },
  "defaultEnabled": false
}
```

Public CLI structured finding through explicit opt-in, redacted:

```text
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<TEMP_PROOF_ROOT>\missing-state
{"ok":false,"checksRun":1,"checksSkipped":27,"findings":[{"checkId":"core/doctor/state-integrity","severity":"error","message":"State directory is missing. Sessions, credentials, logs, and config are stored there.","path":"<TEMP_PROOF_ROOT>\\missing-state","fixHint":"Run `openclaw doctor --fix` to create the state directory."}]}
EXIT_CODE=1
```

Structured dry-run repair effect source harness, redacted:

```json
{
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/state-integrity",
      "severity": "error",
      "message": "State directory is missing. Sessions, credentials, logs, and config are stored there.",
      "path": "<TEMP_PROOF_ROOT>\\missing-state",
      "fixHint": "Run `openclaw doctor --fix` to create the state directory."
    }
  ],
  "repair": {
    "status": "repaired",
    "changes": [],
    "effects": [
      {
        "kind": "state",
        "action": "would-create-state-dir",
        "target": "<TEMP_PROOF_ROOT>\\missing-state",

      }

```

Observed result after fix:
Default lint selection skips the state-integrity check. `--all` selection includes it. The explicit `--only` public lint command ran exactly one check, emitted one `error` finding for `core/doctor/state-integrity`, and exited 1. The structured repair adapter reported the matching `would-create-state-dir` effect without mutating the missing state directory.

What was not tested:
POSIX config permission live CLI output was not tested on this Windows host; that branch remains covered by the POSIX-only regression because Windows intentionally skips mode diagnostics. Command-wide dry-run report output was not tested because that public reporting surface is planned in #84472, not this PR.

## Notes

The rebased diff is 4 files changed. The latest update resolves ClawSweeper's default-lint compatibility finding by making `core/doctor/state-integrity` opt-in for lint while preserving `--all` and `--only` access.

target.title: fix(gateway): restore codex-cli chat history imports
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `chat.history` only backfilled external CLI transcript history for `claude-cli`, so Codex-backed sessions could look empty
after refresh when the local transcript was missing.
- Why it matters: users could lose visible history in Gateway/Control UI refresh flows even though the Codex CLI transcript still
existed on disk.
- What changed: added Codex CLI transcript parsing + import support, filtered commentary-only Codex history before merge, merged
multiple bound CLI transcripts when backfilling an empty local history, and blocked old CLI backfill after session reset until a new
CLI binding is established.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no changes to live chat send flow, provider execution, or non-CLI session history behavior.

AI-assisted. Testing: targeted tests + `pnpm check` passed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the CLI session history import path only supported `claude-cli`, and the import selection logic only chose one external
transcript source.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no Codex-specific history import coverage in `src/gateway/cli-session-history.test.ts`,
and no guard for reset semantics with preserved CLI bindings.
- Contributing context (if known): Codex transcripts include commentary/final-answer phases and can coexist with other CLI bindings
on the same OpenClaw session.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/gateway/cli-session-history.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.sessions.gateway-server-sessions-a.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/cli-session.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Codex-backed sessions backfill history from the Codex session store when the local transcript is empty.
  - Commentary-only Codex messages do not consume `chat.history` visibility budget before sanitization.
  - Empty local history can merge multiple bound CLI transcript sources.
  - Session reset preserves CLI bindings without re-importing old external CLI history into the fresh session.
  - Rebinding a CLI session clears the reset-time suppression flag.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
  - these tests cover the parser, merge policy, Gateway `chat.history` seam, and reset persistence without needing a full end-to-end
CLI run.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
  - `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts` already covered Claude CLI backfill.
- If no new test is added, why not:
  - N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Codex-backed Gateway chat history now restores prior turns from the Codex CLI session store on refresh when no local transcript is
available.
- Commentary-only Codex progress messages are not surfaced as visible history entries.
- Reset sessions no longer repopulate old external CLI history just because preserved CLI bindings still exist.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[refresh chat] -> [local transcript empty] -> [claude-cli only backfill] -> [codex history missing]

After:
[refresh chat] -> [local transcript empty] -> [merge visible bound CLI transcripts] -> [history restored]
[sessions.reset] -> [preserve CLI binding + suppress old import] -> [fresh empty session stays empty]
```


## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation:
    - N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: codex-cli
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway chat.history
- Relevant config (redacted): session entry with cliSessionBindings["codex-cli"].sessionId

### Steps

1. Create or reuse a session entry whose local transcript is empty, but whose cliSessionBindings points at an existing Codex CLI
   session.
2. Call chat.history for that session.
3. Reset the session and call chat.history again.

### Expected

- Before reset: Codex transcript turns are restored from the external Codex session store.
- After reset: the new session remains empty until new local or newly bound CLI history exists.

### Actual

- Before this fix: Codex-backed refreshes could show empty history because only Claude CLI backfill existed.
- Before this fix: if multiple CLI bindings existed, only one external transcript source was imported.
- During review hardening: reset needed an explicit suppression path so preserved old CLI bindings would not refill the new empty
  session.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
    - pnpm check
    - pnpm test src/agents/cli-session.test.ts
    - pnpm test src/gateway/cli-session-history.test.ts

      project files"

- Edge cases checked:

- What you did not verify:

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex-backed Gateway `chat.history` import now restores visible turns from current Codex rollout `response_item` message records when the OpenClaw local transcript is empty, while preserving commentary filtering, multi-CLI merge behavior, and reset-time import suppression.

target.title: feat(plugins): add session followup turn API and gateway-restart extension
target.body:
## Summary

Add a new `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()` method to the plugin runtime, enabling plugins to schedule proactive agent turns for any session — including cold sessions with no active user interaction.

> **Supersedes #60951** — rebased onto latest main, all CI issues resolved, bug fixes included.

## Problem

Plugins have no way to inject a user-role message into an existing session and trigger an agent turn. The available primitives (`enqueueSystemEvent`, `subagent.run`) either don't initiate turns or create new sessions instead of continuing the original conversation. This blocks use cases like:

- **Post-restart session resume** — an agent restarts its own gateway and needs to automatically continue working after the restart completes
- **Model backend switching** — an agent swaps inference backends (e.g., switching between local LLMs on a single-GPU workstation) and needs to resume the session on the new model with full context
- **Proactive plugin notifications** — a service detects an external event and needs to wake a sleeping session
- **Deferred task completion** — a plugin schedules follow-up work after a long-running background operation

## Solution

### Core API: `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()`

A new `followup` namespace on `PluginRuntimeCore` that:

1. **Reconstructs a full `FollowupRun`** from the session store via `buildFollowupRunForSession()` — resolving agent identity, workspace, provider/model, session file, and delivery context from the persisted `SessionEntry`.

2. **Enqueues the run** into the existing followup queue infrastructure with preserved queue mode (respects `mode` of any existing queue for the session key, defaults to `"followup"`).

3. **Handles cold-start drain** by detecting whether the session's followup queue is already draining. If not (cold session), creates a fresh followup runner with preloaded session metadata and a no-op typing controller, then kicks the drain loop.

```typescript
// Plugin usage
const enqueued = await api.runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn({
  sessionKey: "agent:my-agent:telegram:direct:12345",
  prompt: "Background task complete. Here are the results...",
  source: "my-plugin",
});
```

### Bundled Extension: `gateway-restart`

A reference implementation that demonstrates the followup turn API:

- **Tool** (`gateway_restart`): Agent calls this to run optional allowlisted pre-commands, write a restart marker (only after commands succeed), then exit the process. Systemd (or equivalent) auto-restarts the gateway.

- **Service** (`gateway-restart-watcher`): On startup, checks for the restart marker. If found, calls `enqueueFollowupTurn()` to resume the original session with a completion message. Marker is deleted only after successful enqueue — preserved for retry on failure.

Disabled by default (`enabledByDefault: false`). Opt-in via `plugins.entries.gateway-restart.enabled: true`.

## Files Changed

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.ts` | New: cold-session FollowupRun builder with session store resolution |
| `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.ts` | New: plugin runtime followup namespace with hot/cold session detection |
| `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` | Add `followup` type to `PluginRuntimeCore` |
| `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` | Wire `createRuntimeFollowup()` |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/index.ts` | New: gateway-restart plugin (tool + watcher service) |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/openclaw.plugin.json` | New: plugin manifest |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/package.json` | New: package metadata |
| `test/helpers/plugins/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` | Add `followup` to mock |
| `**/**.test.ts` | 24 unit tests across 3 test files |

## Bug Fixes (vs #60951)

1. **Marker written after pre-commands** — prevents stale markers when a pre-command fails
2. **Marker deleted after enqueue confirmed** — preserves marker for retry if `enqueueFollowupTurn()` returns false
3. **Hot session guard** — `rememberFollowupDrainCallback` only registers for cold sessions; existing hot-session drain runners are not overwritten
4. **Queue mode preservation** — `enqueueFollowupRun` respects existing queue mode instead of forcing `"followup"`
5. **Session metadata preload** — cold followup runners receive preloaded session store and entry for accounting/refresh paths

## Testing

- 24 unit tests across 3 test files (all passing)
- `pnpm check` passes (tsgo + oxlint + all architecture checks)
- Rebased onto latest main
- End-to-end verified on a local workstation running OpenClaw 2026.4.8 with Gemma 4 26B via llama.cpp

## Use Cases This Enables

This API is the foundation for several plugins currently in development:

- **`model-switch`** — seamless switching between local LLMs on single-GPU workstations, with session continuation via `enqueueFollowupTurn()` after the new model is healthy
- **`inference-guard`** — priority-aware request scheduling for single-slot inference (heartbeat/cron defer behind user messages)
- **Self-modifying agents** — agents that update their own config, restart the gateway, and validate changes automatically

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm check` passes
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run extensions/gateway-restart/index.test.ts` — 6 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] Gateway-restart tool writes marker only after pre-commands succeed
- [ ] Watcher service enqueues followup turn and deletes marker on success
- [ ] Watcher preserves marker on enqueue failure

## AI Disclosure

This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude Code). Testing level: **fully tested** — 24 unit tests, `pnpm check` green, end-to-end verified on local hardware. The author understands all code in this PR and can explain design decisions.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(exec): expand leading ~ in workdir to home directory
target.body:
## Summary

`exec` tool does not expand a leading `~` in `workdir`, then falls back to another directory and still executes the command, causing relative commands to run from the wrong location.

**Root cause:** `resolveWorkdir()` in `src/agents/bash-tools.shared.ts` passed `workdir` directly to `statSync()` without expanding `~`/`~/` to the user's home directory.

**Fix:** Add leading tilde expansion at the top of `resolveWorkdir()` before the stat check, using `os.homedir()`.

Fixes #94434

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: `resolveWorkdir("~/nonexistent/path")` now expands `~/` to the home directory before the stat check, instead of treating the tilde as a literal path component.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Linux x86_64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node v24.13.1, OpenClaw worktree at `6db0fb3197`

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
node --import tsx -e '
import { resolveWorkdir } from "./src/agents/bash-tools.shared.ts";
const w = [];
console.log("~/path →", resolveWorkdir("~/nonexistent", w), "| warnings:", w.length > 0 ? "yes" : "no");
w.length = 0;
console.log("~ →", resolveWorkdir("~", w), "| warnings:", w.length > 0 ? "yes" : "no");
w.length = 0;
console.log("/tmp →", resolveWorkdir("/tmp", w), "| warnings:", w.length > 0 ? "yes" : "no");
'
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
~/path → /home/0668001315/nonexistent | warnings: yes
~ → /home/0668001315 | warnings: no
/tmp → /tmp | warnings: no
```

**Observed result after the fix**: When a tool call passes `workdir: "~/path"`, the tilde is expanded to the user's home directory before the stat check — if the expanded path exists it is used directly; if not, the fallback warning now shows the expanded path (not the literal `~`).

**What was not tested**: Full end-to-end exec tool invocation through Gateway (requires a live agent session with a model that emits `workdir: "~..."`).

## Tests and validation

```
$ pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  37 passed (37)
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)
- `exec.workdir` with leading `~`/`~/` now expands to home directory instead of failing

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)
- Tilde expansion only affects paths starting with `~`; uses `os.homedir()` which has no side effects

What is the highest-risk area?
- Edge case where homedir() returns an unexpected path

How is that risk mitigated?
- If the expanded path does not exist, the existing fallback mechanism still applies
- The fix only triggers on leading `~` or `~/`, leaving all other path forms unchanged

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review
